one_line_takeaway: SEAL embeds LLM ownership watermarks by aligning latent representations of high-confidence factual anchors with orthogonal bit vectors (rather than creating behavioral backdoors), achieving perfect lineage AUC (1.00) and low BER (0.95% white-box / 1.21% black-box average) against SFT/PEFT/distillation/quantization/merging attacks via a white-box cosine-similarity check or a novel black-box Bayesian Reanchoring procedure that corrects for post-deployment model drift.
key_finding: Across all 6 LLMs, SEAL achieves perfect lineage-identification AUC and pAUC of 1.00 in both white-box and black-box settings (Table 2), whereas black-box baselines (LLMMap, MET, SEF, Trap, SFP, IF-sft, IF-emb) degrade toward random-guessing AUC (~0.5 or below) under the same evaluation, and white-box baselines such as Gradient are inconsistent (e.g., 0.53 AUC on LLaMA2-7B). For multi-bit extraction under the five removal attacks (Table 3), SEAL's average BER across all attacks and bit lengths is 0.69%, with per-attack maxima of 1.51% under SFT (white-box), up to 1.21% BER under PEFT/black-box at 256 bits, up to 0.39% under quantization, and model merging being the hardest attack at up to 4.10% BER (256-bit, black-box). The paper's headline aggregate robustness numbers, repeated in the abstract/intro/conclusion, are 0.95% average BER in white-box settings and 1.21% in black-box settings. Reliable extraction is demonstrated up to 1024 bits (Fig. 3), with the black-box wrong-bit count staying at 0-3 bits absolute even as N scales exponentially. Fidelity: average performance deviation from the unwatermarked model across 15 tasks and 3 models rises from 0.022 (64 bits) to 0.058 (512 bits) (Table 4), described by the authors as negligible. Efficiency: white-box SEAL completes insertion+verification in 34.35s (verification alone: 0.027s, ~500x faster than Huref's 62.94s single-step verification); black-box SEAL completes in 35.93s versus Trap's 3391.18s; GPU memory is ~1.6GB versus up to 72GB for Gradient and 68GB for Trap (Table 5, Fig. 5). Ablations (Sec 6.5) show: Bayesian reanchoring is essential (without it BER ranges 4.7-15.6% across models; with it, 0-1.56%, Table 6); high-confidence anchor selection outperforms low-confidence/random (post-SFT black-box BER 1.56% vs 15.6% vs 6.25%, Table 7); deeper and multi-layer/cross-depth insertion (e.g., layers 5,15,30 combined) is far more robust in the black-box setting than shallow single-layer insertion (Table 8). Under the 'knowledgeable attacker' who fine-tunes exactly the known insertion layers (Sec 7, Table 9), white-box BER stays at 0.000 in all tested cases, and black-box BER rises only modestly (up to 3.125% for some layer groups, 0.000% for cross-depth layer choices), which the authors attribute to the attacker still lacking the secret anchor samples and bit vectors needed to replicate the alignment.
core_contribution: SEAL is a subspace-anchored LLM watermarking framework that embeds multi-bit ownership signatures into a model's latent representation space rather than into its output behavior. Using closed-form model-editing (ME) updates (building on the ROME/MEMIT-style rank-one update formulation in Eq. 1-5, which the paper explicitly attributes to prior work and does not claim as novel), SEAL selects factual anchor triples (subject, relation, object) that the base model already predicts with high confidence, and aligns the hidden representation of each anchor's subject token at layer L with one of two orthogonal per-bit vectors (constructed via Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization) while explicitly preserving the model's original factual prediction via a joint objective (cross-entropy for the original answer + KL divergence + MSE toward the target bit vector, Eq. 9-11). This yields an N-bit watermark encoded across N anchor samples. The paper contributes: (1) the watermark-in-correct-knowledge paradigm itself (as opposed to backdoor/behavioral-anomaly watermarking); (2) a dual verification scheme -- white-box verification via direct cosine-similarity comparison of hidden states to bit vectors (Sec 4.2), and black-box verification via a novel 'Bayesian Reanchoring' procedure (Sec 4.3) that estimates per-bit influence on a small set of 'sentinel token' output logits before deployment, then uses paired-difference drift estimation plus a closed-form Bayesian posterior-mean update (Eq. 15-23) to recover the effective sentinel weights after the model has been fine-tuned/distilled/quantized/merged; (3) an empirical study across 6 LLMs (LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B, Qwen2.5-7B, Qwen2.5-14B, Mistral-7B, DeepSeek-Chat-7B) comparing SEAL against 11 existing fingerprinting/watermarking baselines under 5 categories of removal attacks.
threat_model: Defender: owns a proprietary LLM and, prior to public release, embeds an N-bit watermark to support later lineage identification and ownership verification, either via a leaked/obtained checkpoint (white-box: full weight and hidden-state access) or via API-only query access to next-token logits for a small predefined sentinel-token set (black-box). Attacker: has full access to the released model's parameters and seeks to erase the watermark through standard model-modification operations while preserving general model utility -- specifically five attack families are evaluated: supervised fine-tuning (SFT, full-parameter, on Alpaca/UltraChat), parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT/LoRA), knowledge distillation (student distilled from a teacher), 8-bit symmetric per-tensor quantization, and model merging (linear interpolation, alpha=0.7, with a fine-tuned counterpart). The paper additionally defines a stronger 'knowledgeable attacker' (Sec 7) who knows the SEAL mechanism itself and can infer which layers were used for insertion (though not the secret anchor samples or bit vectors), and fine-tunes specifically those layers to try to erase the signal. The defender's secret key is explicitly scoped: the anchor sample set, the orthogonal bit vectors, and (for black-box) the sentinel token set and reference dataset used for Bayesian reanchoring -- all assumed inaccessible/uninferable by the attacker.
limitations: The paper's own stated boundary conditions and scope limits, drawn from its text rather than inferred: (1) the 'knowledgeable attacker' evaluated in Sec 7 is defined as knowing the SEAL mechanism and the insertion layers, but explicitly NOT the secret anchor samples or bit vectors -- an attacker with knowledge of (or ability to guess) the anchor set is not evaluated, and the paper's own robustness argument for Sec 7 rests on this asymmetry, meaning the claimed resilience to 'knowledgeable attackers' does not cover an adversary who can identify or approximate the anchor triples. (2) The default attack-layer assumption for SFT/PEFT/DT (layers 5-10, 'shallow') is described by the authors as 'a reasonable assumption' based on their own observation that shallow-layer modification maximizes impact on output logits -- it is a paper-chosen convention, not a proof that real-world attackers target these layers. (3) Attack simulations are relatively narrow within each category: SFT uses only Alpaca and UltraChat, quantization is only tested at 8-bit symmetric per-tensor fake quantization, and model merging uses a single interpolation coefficient (alpha=0.7) with only one type of fine-tuned counterpart model -- broader attack hyperparameter/method diversity is not explored. (4) Black-box verification assumes API access to next-token logits for a small predefined sentinel-token set, which the authors acknowledge is 'a realistic assumption' but which is not universal across commercial LLM APIs (e.g., APIs that only return top-k tokens or sampled text, not full logits, would not directly support this). (5) The reported fidelity evaluation (Table 4) is conducted on only 3 of the 6 evaluated models (LLaMA2-7B, LLaMA3-8B, DeepSeek-Chat-7B), not all six. (6) Watermark capacity is only tested up to 1024 bits; the authors state this is a practical choice rather than an architectural ceiling, but no failure point or capacity limit is empirically identified.

datasets_used: CounterFact (source of factual anchor triples for watermark embedding)
MMLU, BoolQ, Winogrande, WSC, COPA, PiQA, OpenBookQA, ARC-Challenge (general knowledge/reasoning fidelity benchmarks)
SST-2, CoLA, MRPC, RTE, NLI (sentence-level understanding fidelity benchmarks)
quantitative_results: Lineage identification AUC/pAUC = 1.00 for SEAL(W) and SEAL(B) across all 6 tested LLMs (Table 2)
Average BER across all 5 attack types and multiple bit lengths = 0.69% (Table 3 summary)
Headline aggregate robustness: 0.95% average BER in white-box settings, 1.21% average BER in black-box settings (Abstract/Conclusion)
Max BER under SFT = 1.51%; max under PEFT (black-box, 256 bits) = 1.21%; max under quantization = 0.39%; max under model merging (black-box, 256 bits) = 4.10% (Table 3 / RQ2 text)
Fidelity: average performance deviation vs. unwatermarked model rises from 0.022 (64-bit) to 0.058 (512-bit) across 15 tasks / 3 models (Table 4)
Runtime: SEAL(W) 34.35s total (0.027s verification only) vs. Huref 62.94s verification; SEAL(B) 35.93s vs. Trap 3391.18s (Table 5)
GPU memory: SEAL ~1.6GB vs. Gradient 72.04GB (white-box) and Trap 67.84GB (black-box) (Figure 5)
Without Bayesian reanchoring BER = 4.688%-15.625% across 5 LLMs; with reanchoring, BER = 0.000%-1.563% (Table 6)
Anchor-selection ablation: high-confidence anchors give post-SFT black-box BER of 1.563% vs. 15.625% (low-confidence) and 6.250% (random) (Table 7)

Knowledgeable-attacker test (Sec 7, Table 9): white-box BER = 0.000% in all tested attack-layer configurations; black-box BER up to 3.125% for some configurations, 0.000% for cross-depth layer choice (5,15,30)
baselines_compared: IF / Instructional Fingerprinting -- IF-sft and IF-emb variants (backdoor-based watermarking)
